key_insight: AI is being used across the cardiac amyloidosis diagnostic pathway, with the strongest progress in binary detection and tracer-burden quantification, while subtype classification, prognosis, and treatment-response monitoring are still early-stage.
clinical_relevance: The main potential clinical value is earlier, more objective case-finding and multimodal interpretation to support distinguishing transthyretin from light-chain cardiac amyloidosis.
method: ECG, echocardiography, CMR, and nuclear imaging models for screening and detection
SPECT/CT biomarker quantification and outcome-linked tracer-burden measurement
Health-record-based case finding plus prognostic and treatment-response modeling
evidence_signal: The abstract emphasizes that the most mature studies use large externally validated cohorts and interpretable, outcome-linked measurements, whereas later-stage tasks rely on smaller, retrospective, heterogeneous datasets.
watch_item: High discrimination alone is not enough; limited external validation, uncertain calibration in real-world prevalence settings, and heterogeneous labels could slow implementation.
paper_title: Artificial Intelligence Across the Cardiac Amyloidosis Diagnostic Pathway: From Single-Modality Detection to Multimodal Clinical Integration
